title-group.article-title: Systematic review of the tuberculosis burden in prisons: Regional patterns and gaps in Latin America and the Caribbean

contrib-group.aff: aEscuela de Ciencias de la Salud, Universidad Autónoma de Baja California, Campus Ensenada, Mexico
bFacultad de Medicina y Nutrición, Universidad Autónoma de Baja California, Campus Mexicali, Mexico
cFacultad de Medicina y Psicología, Universidad Autónoma de Baja California, Campus Tijuana, Mexico
dClínica y Laboratorio de Tuberculosis, Hospital General de Tijuana, IMSS Bienestar, Baja California, Mexico
eFacultad de Ingeniería, Arquitectura y Diseño, Universidad Autónoma de Baja California, Campus Ensenada, Mexico
fFacultad de Humanidades y Ciencias Sociales, Universidad Autónoma de Baja California, Campus Tijuana, Mexico
gPrograma de Micobacteriosis, Secretaría de Salud de Baja California, Tijuana, Baja California, Mexico

fn.p: Red Multidisciplinaria de Investigación en Tuberculosis (RemiTB; www.remitb.org).
Ricardo Perea-Jacobo and José Luis Soto-Ledesma contributed equally to this work.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: To systematically review and synthesize the available literature on the prevalence and incidence of tuberculosis in incarcerated populations in Latin America and the Caribbean, identifying regional patterns, data gaps, and key challenges and providing recommendations for strengthening TB control strategies within prison settings.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: A systematic review was conducted across databases and reports using the following search terms: “tuberculosis”, “prisons”, “prisoners”, “Latin America”, “Caribbean”, “inmates”, and “social readaptation center”. The review focused on prevalence rates, country-specific studies, total prison population studied by country, diagnostic methods used, and the frequency of TB reporting.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: A total of 45 studies met the inclusion criteria, with most conducted in Brazil (60%), Colombia (13%), and Paraguay (9%). TB prevalence and incidence in prisons were found to be up to ten times higher than in the general population, with substantial variation in study design, diagnostic tools, and reporting standards. Structural risk factors such as overcrowding, HIV coinfection, and limited access to molecular diagnostics were frequently reported. Notably, no studies were found from Caribbean countries, and only one study was identified in Mexico, revealing significant regional data gaps.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: TB in Latin American prisons represents a serious but underreported public health crisis. The combination of elevated incidence, limited diagnostics, and fragmented surveillance highlights the urgent need for standardized, prison-specific TB control strategies and expanded research in underrepresented regions.

list-item.p: TB prevalence in prisons is up to 10× higher than in general populations in LAC.
Most studies were concentrated in Brazil, Colombia, and Paraguay.
Diagnostic methods remain mostly conventional, despite some use of molecular tools.
Structural factors like overcrowding and HIV coinfection drive high TB rates.
Heterogeneous data limit comparability; standardization in surveillance is needed.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Prisons are hotspots for tuberculosis (TB) transmission due to a combination of structural, social, and health-related factors, including overcrowding, poverty, undernutrition, HIV infection, poor ventilation, inadequate lighting, and unsanitary conditions. TB incidence is significantly higher in prison populations than in the general population. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), the TB burden in prisons is approximately ten times higher than in the general population, with some regions exhibiting even greater disparities [1,2]. Between 2011 and 2017, TB case notifications among people deprived of liberty quadrupled in Central America—from 538 to 2489 cases—and more than doubled in South America—from 7798 to 17,285 cases [3]. In South America alone, TB incidence in prisons has been reported to be nearly 27 times higher than in the general population [4].

Access to healthcare services within prisons is inadequate, hindering timely diagnosis and effective treatment of TB. Coinfection with TB and HIV is common in many regions, complicating clinical management. Although some Latin American prisons have implemented TB control programs, their effectiveness is often compromised by poor infrastructure and a lack of trained personnel. Additionally, Latin America has reported an increase in both incidence and mortality from TB in the general population, likely to reflect the situation in prisons in the region. Besides, the diabetes pandemic is a health problem mainly for Latin American populations, including the prison population [5].

The WHO's End TB Strategy (2014–2035) outlines a global plan to reduce TB incidence by 90% and TB-related deaths by 95% by 2035, compared to 2015 levels. The strategy is based on three pillars: integrated, patient-centered care and prevention; bold policies and supportive systems; and intensified research and innovation. The framework highlights the urgent need for targeted interventions in high-risk settings such as prisons, where structural deficiencies and health system gaps increase TB transmission [6]. However, surveillance of TB in prisons faces several obstacles, including inefficient data systems, stigma and fear among inmates that discourage symptom reporting, and weak coordination between prison health units and national TB programs. Furthermore, TB reporting procedures and data quality vary significantly across countries and correctional institutions.

Given these challenges, it is critical to conduct studies beyond documenting disease burden and instead focus on identifying regional patterns, highlighting gaps in data availability, and uncovering the structural and systemic obstacles—such as data fragmentation, stigma, limited diagnostic access, and weak health system coordination—that hinder TB prevention and control in prison settings. Such evidence is essential to inform comprehensive and context-sensitive policies, optimize resource allocation, and strengthen TB control strategies in carceral environments throughout Latin America and the Caribbean. Accordingly, the objective of this study was to systematically review and synthesize the available literature on the prevalence and incidence of TB in incarcerated populations across the region, while identifying regional patterns, data gaps, and structural barriers to effective TB prevention and control in prison settings.

sec.title: Search strategy
sec.p: A systematic review of the literature was conducted following the PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses) guidelines to ensure methodological rigor and transparency in reporting [7]. A comprehensive literature search was conducted across electronic databases: MEDLINE (via PubMed), Scopus, Web of Science Core Collection. The search strategy combined three concept blocks using Boolean operators: tuberculosis terms, prison/incarceration terms, and Latin American and Caribbean countries. Both MeSH terms and free-text keywords were employed where appropriate, with no language restrictions applied, though studies were limited to those published from 1999 to 2024 to ensure contemporary relevance. The PubMed search strategy exemplified this approach by combining tuberculosis terms (“Tuberculosis”[Mesh] OR “tuberculosis”[tiab] OR “TB”[tiab]) with incarceration terms (“Prisons”[Mesh] OR “Prisoners”[Mesh] OR “prisons”[tiab] OR “inmates”[tiab] OR “incarcerated”[tiab] OR “correctional facilities”[tiab] OR “penitentiary”[tiab]) and geographic terms (Latin America[Mesh] OR “Latin America”[tiab] OR “South America”[tiab] OR “Central America”[tiab] OR Caribbean[tiab] plus specific country names). The search was supplemented by manual screening of reference lists from included studies.

sec.title: Eligibility criteria
sec.p: Eligibility criteria included original studies with primary data on incarcerated individuals in Latin America/Caribbean. While some studies also included data on prison staff, only data related to incarcerated populations were extracted and analyzed. Studies had to report TB incidence and/or prevalence data and provide a clear description of diagnostic methods (smear microscopy, culture, molecular tests -Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) or GeneXpert). Both confirmed TB cases and presumptive cases based on clinical or radiological criteria were included. The search covered publications from January 1995 to July 2024, in any language, provided they met the inclusion criteria.

sec.title: Exclusion criteria
sec.p: Studies were excluded if employed case-control design, and included participants under 18 years of age and lacked primary data collection.

sec.title: Information processing
sec.p: After executing the search strategy, two reviewers independently screened the retrieved articles. Duplicate records were identified and removed using reference management software. The remaining studies underwent a two-stage selection process: (1) title and abstract screening to identify potentially relevant studies, and (2) a full-text review to determine final inclusion based on predefined eligibility criteria.

sec.title: Data extraction
sec.p: Data from included studies were systematically extracted using a standardized form. The following information was collected from each article: first author and year of publication, country, journal, study design, total sample size, study period, prevalence or incidence data, diagnostic methods employed (smear microscopy, culture, molecular tests, radiography, tuberculin skin test, clinical evaluation), and sociodemographic characteristics of the prison population (age, sex, HIV prevalence, overcrowding rates, drug use, alcohol consumption, educational level, and time in prison). When available, we also extracted data on temporal trends and risk factors associated with tuberculosis in prison settings.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p:
Descriptive statistics were used to summarize the characteristics of included studies (Table 1). For each country, we calculated the total number of studies, the combined study period, the aggregate sample size, and the predominant study designs. Not all included studies contributed data to both prevalence and incidence analyses. Prevalence estimates were primarily derived from cross-sectional studies, while incidence data came from cohort and time-series studies. Observational studies were classified based on their specific design features: those with a single time-point assessment contributed to prevalence estimates, while those with longitudinal follow-up provided incidence data. Time-series studies that tracked new TB cases over multiple years were used exclusively for incidence calculations. Studies focused on latent tuberculosis infection (LTBI) rather than active TB disease were excluded from active TB prevalence calculations.Table 1
Characteristics of the studies included in the systematic review.Table 1
CountryNo. of studiesTotal periodTotal sample sizePredominant study types (N)
Brazil272000–2020974,994Cross-sectional (12), Retrospective cohort (10), Prospective (3), Mixed methods (1), Ecological (1).
Colombia62010–2018126,525Cross-sectional (2)Prospective (2)Longitudinal (1), Ecological (1)
Paraguay42009–20216841Retrospective (3)Prospective (1)
Perú32016–202169,890Cross-sectional (1)Retrospective (2)
Ecuador32015–20191747Cross-sectional (2)Retrospective (1)
El Salvador12009–20141177Retrospective cohort (1)
México11999–20005375Cross-sectional (1)
Note: Latin American and Caribbean countries without published TB prevalence or incidence data in prison settings during the study period (1999–2024) include: Argentina, Belize, Bolivia, Chile, Costa Rica, Cuba, Dominica, Dominican Republic, Grenada, Guatemala, Guyana, Haiti, Honduras, Jamaica, Nicaragua, Panama, Saint Lucia, Suriname, Trinidad and Tobago, Uruguay, and Venezuela.

For prevalence analysis (Table 2), we extracted or calculated the point prevalence of active TB in each study. When studies reported prevalence per 100,000 population, these were converted to percentages. The prevalence ratio (PR) was calculated asPrevalence Ratio=Prevalence in prison populationPrevalence in general populationx100where:Prevalence in prison population=Number ofTBcases in prisonTotal prison population screenedx100,000Table 2
Prevalence of tuberculosis in prisons in Latin America.Table 2
CountryNumber. of studiesPeriodPrevalence interval (%)Median (%)Prevalence ratio⁎⁎⁎
Brazil162002-20210.40 - 8.602.8510.4–198.3
Colombia32010–20151.03–5.503.2740.4–205.7
Ecuador42015–20191.66–3.951.9857.8–157.8
México11999–20002.462.46133.1
Paraguay12013–202115.1015.10373.8
Perú120162.512.5184.2⁎
Number of studies providing active TB prevalence data.⁎⁎
Prevalence ratio: TB prevalence in prison population divided by TB prevalence in the general population (per 100,000), based on WHO/PAHO data for the corresponding country and year.

Prevalence in general population = TB prevalence reported by the WHO or the Pan American Health Organization (PAHO) for the corresponding country and year of each study, expressed per 100,000 population.

For each country, we reported the prevalence interval (minimum and maximum values across all studies), the median prevalence, and the range of prevalence ratios, providing a quantitative measure of the TB burden in prisons relative to the general population. This comparison allows for a standardized assessment of the disproportionate TB burden in prison settings across different Latin American countries and periods. Not all included studies contributed data to both prevalence and incidence analyses. Prevalence estimates were primarily derived from cross-sectional studies, while incidence data came from cohort and time series studies. Observational studies were classified based on their specific design features: those with a single time-point assessment contributed to prevalence estimates, while those with longitudinal follow-up provided incidence data. Time series studies that tracked new TB cases over multiple years were used exclusively for incidence calculations.

The incidence of TB and its disparity with the general population were calculated using longitudinal data, as summarized in Table 3. For each country, we reported the median incidence rate (per 100,000 persons) across studies, its interval, and the corresponding median Rate Ratio (RT). The RT, calculated by dividing the prison incidence by the general population incidence for the same year (based on PAHO reports), quantifies the relative risk.Table 3
Incidence of tuberculosis in prisons in Latin America.Table 3
CountryNumber of studiesPeriodMedian incidence (per 100,000)IntervalMedian RT⁎
Brazil92001–201918,002,03032–378941.8
Colombia52010–20181314505–227240.1
Paraguay42013–202130122272 - 345968.767.7
Ecuador22015–201920192063 - 394747.1
El Salvador120141688–37.5
México11999–20002460–58.4
Perú12018–20212894–24.7⁎
RT: Rate ratio (incidence in prison vs. general population).

sec.title: Quality assessment
sec.p: The methodological quality of included studies was systematically evaluated by two independent reviewers using validated assessment tools. For cohort, and cross-sectional studies, we applied the Newcastle-Ottawa Scale (NOS) [8], which employs a star-based scoring system with a maximum of 9 stars across three domains: selection of study groups, comparability of groups, and ascertainment of exposure or outcome. For descriptive observational and ecological studies where the NOS was not fully applicable, we used the STROBE (Strengthening the Reporting of Observational Studies in Epidemiology) checklist, which assesses reporting quality across 22 items [9]. Quality scores were recorded for each study, and discrepancies between reviewers were resolved through discussion or consultation with a third reviewer. Studies were not excluded based on quality scores; instead, quality assessment was used to inform the interpretation of results and to conduct sensitivity analyses when appropriate (Supplementary S01).

sec.title: Results
sec.p:
A total of 519 records were initially identified through four major databases: Scopus (n = 200), PubMed (n = 166), Web of Science (n = 120), and unspecified sources (n = 33). After removing 255 duplicates (12 manually and 243 through Covidence software), 264 studies remained for title and abstract screening. Of these, 82 full-text articles were assessed for eligibility, with 37 studies excluded for not meeting the inclusion criteria. Ultimately, 45 studies were included in the final review (Fig. 1). The studies encompassed observational quantitative methodologies across seven Latin American countries, where Brazil reported the highest number of studies (27/45) (Table 1, Supplementary S01).Fig. 1
PRISMA 2020 flow diagram for the systematic review of tuberculosis burden in prisons, illustrating the stages from initial article identification to final inclusion in the systematic review.Fig. 1

sec.title: Prevalence and incidence of tuberculosis in prison populations
sec.p: The reported prevalence of TB among prison populations varied substantially by country and study. Paraguay recorded the highest point prevalence at 15.1% [10]. In Colombia, TB prevalence ranged between 1.03% and 5.5%. The most extreme case was reported in Brazil, where TB prevalence in prison was 198.3 times higher than in the general population. Most studies documented prevalence ratios exceeding 50 (Table 2).

The incidence of TB among prison populations in Latin America was found to be alarmingly high across all countries with available data. In Brazil, reported incidence rates ranged from 1044 to 3789 cases per 100,000 inmates per year, with the highest value documented in 2012—equivalent to a rate 782.4 times higher than that of the general population [11]. In Paraguay, incidence ranged between 2272 and 3459 per 100,000, with corresponding rate ratios from 52.8 to 80.4 when compared to the general population [10]. Similarly, Ecuador reported incidence rates between 1974 and 2063 per 100,000 [12,13], while El Salvador showed a sharp increase from 532 to 1688 per 100,000 in the years leading up to 2014 [14]. A longitudinal analysis in Brazil also revealed a steady rise in incidence, from 959 to 1483 per 100,000 over a ten-year period [15]. Overall, these findings demonstrate a concentration of TB transmission in prisons, with incidence rates 25 to 52 times higher than those in surrounding communities (Table 3).

sec.title: Sociodemographic and environmental characteristics of prison populations in included studies
sec.p:
The studies included in this review consistently reported sociodemographic characteristics, health conditions, and environmental factors of the prison populations studied, revealing common patterns across Latin American settings (Table 4). These characteristics provide context for understanding the populations at risk for TB in carceral environments.Table 4
Sociodemographic characteristics of the prison population analyzed.Table 4
CountryNumber of studiesMedian age (years)Median % menMedian % HIVMedian overcrowding (%)
Brazil2730.597.78183
Colombia4301004.2212
Paraguay431.189.23.6n.d.
Perú334.196.82.8220
Ecuador3311006.5197
El Salvador128996.4325
México12893.97.6n.d.
n.d. No data.

sec.title: Demographic characteristics
sec.p: The studies reviewed indicated that the mean age of inmates with TB typically ranged from 28 to 35 years, suggesting that most affected individuals fall within early adulthood. Research from Brazil [16], Colombia [17], and Mexico [18] consistently reported mean ages between 28 and 33 years. Slightly older populations were observed in Paraguay [19]; mean age 35.8 years) and Peru [20]; mean age 35.9 years). Most studies documented predominantly male populations, with 29 studies reporting male proportions exceeding 90%. Exceptions were noted in Paraguay [19]; 63.3% male) and Colombia [21]; 51.6% male.

Educational attainment among inmates was generally low across the region. In Brazil, one study reported that 37.8% of incarcerated individuals had not completed primary education [15], while others indicated that between 49.5% and 65.7% had less than eight years of schooling [19,22]. In Colombia, educational levels appeared slightly higher, with up to 75% of inmates having completed secondary education [17]. In contrast, Paraguay showed particularly concerning figures, with 13.9% of inmates identified as illiterate [23].

sec.title: Health-related factors
sec.p: HIV coinfection among incarcerated individuals with tuberculosis showed marked variability across countries. In Brazil, reported prevalence ranged from below 2% in some settings to over 20% in others [16,24], indicating substantial heterogeneity within the prison system. Studies from Colombia documented intermediate levels of HIV coinfection, with estimates ranging from 2.1% to 12.5% [25,26]. In contrast, considerably lower HIV prevalence was observed in Paraguay (2.4%) [19] and Peru (0.4%) [20].

Drug use was highly prevalent among inmates. In Brazil, reported rates ranged from 18.6% [27] to 58.8% [22]. In Colombia, reported similarly high rates of 62.5% and 61%, respectively [17,28]. The highest prevalence was recorded in Mexico, where 90.9% of inmates reported drug use [18]. Although alcohol use was reported less frequently, it remained significant, with prevalence ranging from 10.1% Brazil [27], to 67.7% Peru [29].

Severe overcrowding was documented in nearly all prison settings. The most extreme case was reported by [30] in Brazil, where occupancy reached 375% of designed capacity. Other studies in Brazil recorded similarly extreme overcrowding rates, including 325% [16], 216% [31], 240.5% [24], and 141.7% [15]. In Ecuador, Chong, F. et al. [13] reported occupancy at 197%, while Peruvian prisons faced a 220% occupancy rate [32] (Table 4).

sec.title: Duration of incarceration
sec.p: Incarceration length varied markedly across the studies included. The shortest median stay was 33 days, reported in a Brazilian facility [30]. Most research indicated prison terms ranging from several months to two years. In Colombia, average incarceration durations reached up to 35 months [28], while other studies documented averages of 14 and 12 months [25,33]. In Ecuador, the average duration was 21 months [13], consistent with findings from Mexico [18]. In Paraguay, the average incarceration period was approximately 2.25 years [19,23].

sec.title: Diagnostic methods
sec.p: Smear microscopy remains the most used diagnostic method in prison settings across Latin America, reported in 88% of the studies. Microbiological culture was also frequently used (63%), particularly in Brazil, Colombia, Ecuador, and Paraguay (Supplementary S01). Clinical evaluation and chest X-ray were employed in 45% and 31% of the studies, respectively, often in combination with other methods.

Notably, molecular diagnostics, such as GeneXpert and MIRU-VNTR were used in only 27% of the studies and reported exclusively in Brazil, Paraguay, and Peru. While GeneXpert is suitable for rapid diagnosis and drug resistance detection, MIRU-VNTR is primarily used for molecular epidemiology (i.e. transmission studies) rather than clinical diagnosis. The skin test and histopathology were the least frequently reported, used mainly in Brazil and Colombia (skin test), and in Brazil, Paraguay, and Peru (histopathology) (Table 5 and Supplementary S01.Table 5
Diagnostic methods used for tuberculosis in prisons.Table 5
MethodNumber of studies%Countries
Smear microscopy3588All Countries
Microbiological Culture3163Brazil, Colombia, Ecuador, Paraguay
Clinical evaluation2245Brazil, Colombia, Ecuador, Mexico, Paraguay, Peru
X-ray1531Brazil, Ecuador, Mexico, Paraguay, Peru
Molecular tests927Brazil, Paraguay, Peru
Skin test612Brazil, Colombia
Self-report (questionnaire)24Brazil, Peru
Histopathology12México

More recent innovations include computer-aided detection for TB (CAD4TB) in chest radiographs, which were applied in Brazilian studies [22,34] (Table 5). These findings highlight the predominance of conventional diagnostic methods in prison settings, with limited but emerging use of molecular technologies.

sec.title: Prevalence and incidence of tuberculosis in prison populations
sec.p: The total number of selected articles reporting TB prevalence and incidence in Latin American prisons between 1999 and 2022 was only 45, slightly more than one article per year for the whole Latin America region. Besides, only 21% of Latin American countries (7/33) have published a research study. This lack of information is not congruent with the TB prevalence in Latin America, which has been reported as increasing [35]. Since 2000, the detection and notification of TB in the prison population in Central and South America has quadrupled, from 538 to 2489 cases (462.6%) and 7798 to 17,285 cases (221.65%), respectively [3]. The strategies to reduce the prevalence of TB in prisons highlighted above should focus on overcrowding, better hygiene, and ventilation, as well as an active search for infection of TB and HIV. Radiological and bacteriological examinations are used to diagnose and follow up on active cases of TB [36].

The largest number of studies identified were from Brazil, with 27 studies; the country with the highest absolute number of TB cases in Latin America reported an estimated 103,000 cases in 2022 (48 cases per 100,000 inmates per year) [35], with 10% co-infected with HIV. It also has the third-largest prison population globally, exceeding 830,000 people in 2024 [37]. Conditions of chronic overcrowding and limited healthcare access have led to significantly elevated TB rates in prisons, with incidence ranging from 1314 to 3012 per 100,000 inmates per year [23,28] and prevalence as high as 8.6% [3,38]. These findings highlight the urgent need for integrated TB control strategies in carceral settings, including early diagnosis, continuity of care post-release, and structural reforms to reduce overcrowding and prevent prison-driven transmission [35].

In Colombia, seven studies on TB in prisons have been identified, reflecting a relatively strong but insufficient research output given the severity of the issue. Active TB prevalence among inmates ranges from 1.03% to 5.5% [25,28]. TB infection rates are also concerning, with a prevalence of 77.6% reported by Rueda, Arroyave [33], indicating widespread exposure to M. tuberculosis. Estimated incidence rates in Colombian prisons may exceed 1500 to 2000 cases per 100,000 inmates (2000–2018), compared to just 25–30 cases per 100,000 in the general population [39]. This means incarcerated individuals face a 50- to 80-fold higher risk of developing TB. These findings emphasize the urgent need for systematic screening, preventive strategies, structural improvements in Colombia's prison system, and continuity of care after release.

In Paraguay, four studies conducted between 2009 and 2021 highlight the country's status as a high TB burden setting in South America. According to the WHO [35], Paraguay reported approximately 5600 (4600–6800) TB new cases in 2024, resulting in an incidence rate of 81 (66–98) cases per 100,000 inmates. The National Ministry of Health has noted a sustained increase in TB cases in recent years, particularly among vulnerable populations, including Indigenous peoples, individuals living with HIV, residents of densely populated urban areas, and people deprived of liberty. A study by Walter, K.S. et al. [3] indicated that TB notification rates in Paraguayan prisons are more than 40 times higher than those in the general population. Further research has revealed an active TB prevalence of up to 15.1% in certain prisons [23], with incidence rates ranging from 2272 to 3459 cases per 100,000 inmates per year. These alarming statistics underscore the urgent need for targeted interventions within the prison system to address this public health crisis and highlight Paraguay's classification as a high TB burden setting in South America.

Although only one study from Mexico met the inclusion criteria for this review, its inclusion is warranted due to the country's epidemiological and strategic relevance in the region. The study, conducted from 1999 to 2000 in a prison in Tijuana, Baja California, focused on a state with a TB incidence of 60.2 cases per 100,000 inhabitants—more than double the national average of 19.6 [40]. Remarkably, this subnational incidence exceeds even the national average reported for Brazil (49 per 100,000; 95% CI: 40–61), a country that not only contributed the largest number of studies in our review but is also the only Latin American country currently listed among the 30 high TB burden countries globally [35]. Baja California also has the highest annual prisoner turnover in Mexico, with over 13,500 admissions per year, which significantly increases the risk of TB transmission both within prisons and to the broader community upon release [41]. This case highlights the importance of analyzing TB data at the subnational level, as reliance on national averages can obscure critical hotspots. The lack of updated research in such a high-burden setting points to a pressing gap in surveillance and control efforts and underscores the need for renewed investment in regional prison-based TB studies in Mexico and across underrepresented areas of Latin America. Besides, Mexico serves as a critical geopolitical link between Central America and North America. Its position at the U.S. border makes it highly relevant for binational TB control efforts, especially given the high incarceration and migration flows in the region [42].

sec.title: Risk factors for tuberculosis in prison populations
sec.p: The high burden of TB in LATAM prisons results from a convergence of structural, demographic, and health-related risk factors that facilitate transmission and complicate disease control. Overcrowding was the most consistently reported factor, with prison occupancy reaching up to 375% of designed capacity in Brazil and exceeding 200% in Ecuador and Peru. These conditions violate international standards and contribute to airborne disease spread through poor ventilation and overcrowded cells [[43], [44], [45], [46], [47], [48]].

Demographically, TB primarily affects young adult males aged 28 to 36 years, reflecting the general prison population [49,50]. However, notable exceptions were found in Paraguay and Colombia, where the proportion of male inmates was lower (63.3% and 51.6%, respectively), underscoring the need to address gender-specific vulnerabilities [51,52]. Educational attainment was low across most settings; in Brazil and Paraguay, a significant proportion of inmates had not completed primary education or were illiterate, which may hinder TB awareness and treatment adherence [[53], [54], [55], [56]].

HIV coinfection was, reported in all countries, with prevalence ranging from 0.4% in Peru to 21.55% in Brazil. These cases represent a significant comorbidity contributing to the reactivation of TB infection and increasing the complexity of treatment [65]. HIV prevalence variations likely reflect differences in screening practices and access to antiretroviral therapy, and regional epidemic dynamics [57,58]. Coinfection complicates TB management and increases the risk of progression to active disease. In some countries, underdiagnosis or stigma may also lead to underreporting.

Substance use, particularly illicit drug use, was prevalent in all settings, reaching 90.9% in Mexico and overs 60% in Colombia. Alcohol use was reported in up to 67.7% of inmates in Peru. These comorbidities can weaken immunity and disrupt TB treatment continuity, which is also significant [[61], [62], [63], [64]].

Incarceration duration varied widely, from short-term stays (33 days) to long-term imprisonment (up to 4 years). Even brief stays in overcrowded facilities increase exposure risk. Frequent turnover among pretrial detainees can sustain TB transmission and limit treatment completion, contributing to drug resistance [4,[59], [60], [61]].

These interrelated factors create a high-risk environment for TB transmission in prisons. Addressing TB in these settings requires integrated interventions, including improved living conditions, targeted screening (e.g., chest X-rays), new technologies (e.g. molecular diagnostic and artificial intelligence algorithms) health treatment, and strengthened continuity of care post-release [10,14].

Understanding these interconnected and reinforcing pathways is essential for the development of comprehensive and multisectoral intervention strategies—ones that not only reduce immediate transmission risks within carceral settings but also tackle the underlying social and systemic determinants that perpetuate TB vulnerability and health inequities [62,63].

sec.title: Diagnostic methods
sec.p: Smear microscopy and microbiological culture were the most frequently used diagnostic methods across the studies included in this review. Only a small proportion (eleven studies) reported the use of molecular methods for TB diagnosis, mainly Xpert MTB/RIF. The WHO recommends rapid molecular diagnostic tests as the initial diagnostic tool for all individuals with signs and symptoms of TB, given their high accuracy and ability to detect drug resistance early [70].

In fact, several molecular platforms beyond Xpert MTB/RIF are now available and demonstrate equal or superior performance, such as BD MAX MDR-TB (Becton Dickinson), RealTime MTB (Abbott), FluoroType MTBDR (Bruker-Hain Diagnostics), Cobas 5800 (Roche) and Anyplex MTB/NTM (Seegene) [64]. A cost-benefit study evaluating four TB screening strategies in incarcerated populations demonstrated that symptom-based screening combined with GeneXpert could detect up to 81.3% of TB cases, and the estimated cost per detected case was approximately USD 255, factoring in consultation time, sample transport, and processing [24,72,73].

MIRU-VNTR (Mycobacterial Interspersed Repetitive Units–Variable Number Tandem Repeat) genotyping has been widely used to investigate M. tuberculosis transmission dynamics [[65], [66], [67]], including in prison settings. This molecular method enables the identification of strain clusters by analyzing polymorphic tandem repeat loci in the bacterial genome, helping to detect recent and old transmission events. In correctional facilities, MIRU-VNTR has proven useful in uncovering TB outbreaks, demonstrating in-prison transmission, and clusters occurring in different penitentiary centers or between inmates and surrounding communities. Several studies have demonstrated that many TB cases within prisons belonged to closely related genetic clusters, indicating ongoing transmission within these confined environments [[68], [69], [70]]. However, while MIRU-VNTR remains a valuable surveillance tool, its resolution is lower than that of more advanced techniques such as WGS, which is increasingly being adopted for high-resolution transmission analysis in high-burden settings like prisons [[71], [72], [73], [74]]. However, in this review, only one study was reported using WGS to analyze transmission, reporting frequent recent transmission within prisons and transmission linkages spanning prisons and surrounding urban populations in Paraguay [71]. Besides, it is important to note that both MIRU-VNTR and WGS involve significantly higher costs and are intended for epidemiological and research purposes rather than routine clinical diagnosis.

It has been reported that approximately 25% of active pulmonary TB cases experience diagnostic delays due to the lack of equipment and trained personnel to perform chest X-rays [71]. Although chest radiography is generally accepted by incarcerated individuals, its use as a screening tool is often limited in resource-constrained settings. Nonetheless, periodic chest X-ray screening is still recommended, adapted to the needs and capacities of each facility, along with educational efforts to help inmates recognize TB symptoms. Notably, the implementation of portable digital radiography and the growing use of artificial intelligence (AI)-assisted interpretation have been promoted to enhance diagnostic efficiency and overcome logistical barriers [35,64,75].

Incarceration settings with limited resources often lack access to adequate drug susceptibility testing (DST), whether phenotypic or molecular. This limitation not only hinders effective clinical management for individuals with TB but also enables the undetected spread of multidrug-resistant TB (MDR-TB) in overcrowded prison environments [3,61]. Frequent inmate turnover, combined with long incubation period of M. tuberculosis, incomplete treatment, and inadequate follow-up after release, creates a high-risk environment where transmission often occurs inside prison but manifests as active disease later in the community. A recent investigation conducted in Peru (2017–2019) revealed that 13% of MDR-TB strains were classified as pre-XDR), and 3.5% had mutations associated with bedaquiline resistance [76]. The study also found a strong association between recent transmission clusters and current or past incarceration, reinforcing the critical role that prisons play in sustaining the MDR-TB epidemic in the region [61]. However, the full extent of TB transmission linked to incarceration remains poorly documented. Although the elevated TB burden in prison populations is increasingly recognized, there is a striking lack of studies addressing how incarceration contributes to the spread of TB within surrounding communities. This gap in understanding is further complicated by limited data access due to confidentiality and security concerns within the correctional system.

The continued reliance on outdated diagnostic tools and insufficient treatment strategies within prisons undermines international TB control efforts. It also goes against WHO guidelines, which recommend the use of rapid molecular tests as the primary diagnostic approach for all suspected TB cases [4,35]. There is a pressing need to expand routine TB screening, implement preventive therapy, and invest in infrastructure improvements to reduce transmission risks. Widening access to molecular diagnostics in correctional facilities is vital not only to control drug resistance but also to safeguard public health both within prisons and in the broader community.

Beyond addressing infrastructure and diagnostic limitations, improving health literacy among incarcerated individuals is essential for the success of TB prevention and treatment programs. Studies show that even when services are available, low health literacy often results in delayed symptom reporting, poor adherence to treatment, and limited participation in health initiatives. For example, Lochmannova, A. [54] highlights how targeted interventions to build health knowledge and communication skills among inmates significantly improved engagement with TB services and treatment outcomes. Our findings underscore the importance of including cultural and linguistically appropriate education strategies, particularly in settings with high levels of illiteracy or low educational attainment. Finally, the lack of published studies from many countries in the region may reflect structural research barriers rather than an absence of TB. Strategic investments in national research capacity, regional collaborations, and standardized TB surveillance protocols are essential to close these data gaps and enable more equitable and effective responses.

sec.title: Limitations of the study
sec.p: This study has several limitations. The most notable is the methodological heterogeneity across studies, including differences in diagnostic methods, case definitions, and study designs, which complicate comparisons and prevent meta-analysis. Geographic coverage was also limited to seven countries, reducing regional representativeness. Potential publication and language biases and variability in data quality regarding risk factors and comorbidities were identified. The long study period (1999–2024) introduces temporal bias, and underrepresentation of incarcerated women and prison staff further constrains the findings. Missing data were reported as “Not Available” (NA) in tables and were not imputed. Studies with incomplete reporting were included in the descriptive synthesis, with their limitations explicitly noted. Outliers, particularly extreme incidence or prevalence rates, were retained because they may reflect actual epidemiological conditions in specific carceral environments. These values were contextualized in the discussion to prevent misinterpretation.
These limitations underscore the need for more standardized, regionally comprehensive research to fully characterize TB in prison settings across Latin America and the Caribbean.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This systematic review confirms a disproportionately high burden of TB in prisons across Latin America and the Caribbean, with prevalence and incidence rates up to 100 times higher than in the general population. However, scientific evidence remains scarce, fragmented, and concentrated in only a few countries—most notably Brazil and Colombia—leaving critical gaps in surveillance and research coverage. This lack of data does not reflect an absence of disease but rather institutional and structural shortcomings in health monitoring and policy prioritization.

The findings reveal significant heterogeneity in study designs, diagnostic approaches, and reporting practices, which impedes meaningful regional comparisons and coordinated public health action. Persistent underdiagnosis is driven by limited access to rapid molecular tests, inconsistent TB screening protocols, and poor integration between prison health services and national TB programs. Addressing the tuberculosis crisis in Latin American prisons requires coordinated, multisectoral action. Researchers should focus on conducting studies in underrepresented countries and populations—such as women and prison staff—while using standardized methodologies to enable cross-context comparisons. Policymakers must prioritize TB surveillance in carceral settings as both a public health and human rights imperative by allocating resources to strengthen diagnostic infrastructure, including portable radiography and molecular platforms. Practitioners play a critical role in implementing routine and context-appropriate screening strategies, training prison health personnel, and promoting culturally and linguistically tailored health literacy interventions to improve treatment adherence. Finally, regional collaboration among ministries of health, justice, academic institutions, and civil society is essential to close knowledge gaps, harmonize surveillance systems, and ensure continuity of care following incarceration.

Without urgent and coordinated efforts, prisons will continue to act as reservoirs that perpetuate TB transmission both inside and outside carceral facilities. Closing these gaps is essential to achieve equitable and effective TB control strategies aligned with the Global Plan to End TB and the Sustainable Development Goals.

sec.title: What does this study contribute to existing knowledge?
sec.p: This systematic review offers a comprehensive synthesis of TB in Latin American prisons, revealing the significantly higher burden of TB among incarcerated populations compared to the general population. It highlights critical gaps in surveillance and research, with many countries lacking any published studies despite having large, incarcerated populations. The study emphasizes the need to view TB in prisons as both a public health crisis and a reflection of broader social and structural inequities, deepening our understanding of how these environments contribute to disease transmission and progression.

sec.title: What are the key implications for public health interventions, practice, or policy?
sec.p: The findings underscore the need for integrated multisectoral strategies to address TB in prisons, including systematic screening, expanded diagnostic capacity (rapid molecular and AI-assisted digital X-rays), improved facility conditions, and integration of prison health services with national TB programs. Structural reforms to reduce pretrial detention and implement alternative sentencing are also critical to mitigate overcrowding and transmission risks. All interventions must align with international human rights standards to protect the health and dignity of incarcerated individuals and support regional and global TB elimination efforts.

sec.title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
sec.p: Ricardo Perea-Jacobo: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Jose Luis Soto-Ledesma: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Rafael Laniado-Laborín: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Investigation. Dora-Luz Flores: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision. Rogelio Zapata-Garibay: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation. J. Eduardo González-Fagoaga: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation. Luis Alberto García-Sánchez: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation. Javier Robles-Flores: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Liliana Guadalupe Villa-Aviles: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Data curation. Raquel Muñiz-Salazar: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization.

sec.p: Not applicable.

sec.p: Not applicable.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: This research was partially supported by the 10.13039/501100007228Universidad Autónoma de Baja California (Project Number 3717) and by the 10.13039/100005595AlianzaMx–University of California initiative (2024–2026).

sec.title: Declaration of competing interest
sec.p: We confirm that neither the manuscript nor any part of its content is currently under consideration for publication elsewhere, nor has it been published previously.
